title: Senior React Engineer Specialist Mock Test
description: An advanced-level assessment focused on React 18+ architecture, fiber reconciliation, performance optimization, and complex state synchronization patterns.

questions.text: How does React 18's 'Automatic Batching' handle state updates inside promises or setTimeouts compared to previous versions?
options: It only batches updates inside React event handlers.
It batches all updates regardless of where they originate (promises, timeouts, native events).
It requires the use of flushSync to enable batching for async code.
It disables batching for async code to ensure data integrity.
questions.explanation: React 18 introduced automatic batching for all updates, reducing unnecessary re-renders in asynchronous contexts.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the `useSyncExternalStore` hook?
options: To synchronize local state with an API.
To subscribe to external data stores while ensuring compatibility with Concurrent Rendering (preventing 'tearing').
To provide a synchronous way to access the Context API.
To replace the Redux 'connect' function in functional components.
questions.explanation: This hook is crucial for external state management libraries to remain consistent during concurrent updates.

questions.text: In the Fiber architecture, what is the 'Double Buffering' technique?
options: Using two different versions of the Virtual DOM (current and workInProgress) to prepare updates off-screen.
Rendering components twice to detect side effects.
Storing state in both memory and local storage for persistence.
A mechanism for handling two simultaneous user inputs.
questions.explanation: React uses a 'current' tree and a 'workInProgress' tree to perform non-blocking rendering without showing an incomplete UI.

questions.text: What does the `useDeferredValue` hook accomplish that a standard debounce function does not?
options: It adds a fixed delay to every update.
It is interruptible and only triggers the update when the main thread is idle, yielding to urgent work.
It prevents the component from rendering until the value is ready.
It automatically memoizes the component tree.
questions.explanation: Unlike debouncing, useDeferredValue is integrated into React's scheduler and yields to user interactions.

questions.text: Which optimization is essential for a component wrapped in `React.memo` that receives a callback function as a prop?
options: Using useMemo on the component's state.
Wrapping the callback in useCallback in the parent component.
Adding a key prop to the component.
Using useLayoutEffect inside the component.
questions.explanation: If the callback isn't memoized, its reference changes on every parent render, defeating the purpose of React.memo.

questions.text: What occurs during the 'Commit' phase of the React lifecycle?
options: React calculates the diff between the old and new tree.
React applies changes to the DOM and calls lifecycle methods/Layout Effects.
React decides which updates are high priority.
The browser paints the pixels on the screen.
questions.explanation: The render phase is interruptible and side-effect free; the commit phase is where actual DOM changes occur and is synchronous.

questions.text: What is 'Hydration' in the context of React Server Components and SSR?
options: Loading CSS files into the head of the document.
The process of attaching event listeners to server-rendered HTML to make it interactive.
Connecting the frontend to a GraphQL server.
Refreshing the data in a cache.
questions.explanation: Hydration 'waters' the dry HTML sent from the server by attaching the React runtime logic.

questions.text: How can you prevent a 'Stale Closure' in a `useEffect` hook that uses a variable from the component scope?
options: By using a useRef for the variable.
By including the variable in the effect's dependency array.
By removing the dependency array entirely.
Both A and B are valid strategies depending on the use case.
questions.explanation: Including it in the array ensures the effect re-runs with new values; using a ref ensures the effect always sees the latest mutable value without re-running.

questions.text: What is the specific use case for `useInsertionEffect`?
options: Inserting elements into a list efficiently.
Injecting dynamic styles for CSS-in-JS libraries before other layout effects run.
Optimizing the performance of portals.
Handling ref assignments during the render phase.
questions.explanation: It fires before all layout effects and is primarily intended for library authors to inject <style> tags.

questions.text: In a Redux architecture, what is 'Normalized State'?
options: Storing data in a flat structure (like a database) to avoid deeply nested objects.
Converting all numbers to strings.
Using a single state object for the entire app.
Ensuring state matches the backend schema exactly.
questions.explanation: Normalization makes updates more efficient and prevents data duplication across the state tree.

questions.text: What does the `flushSync` utility do?
options: It clears the browser cache.
It forces React to exit automatic batching and apply updates immediately/synchronously.
It synchronizes the state across multiple browser tabs.
It performs a hard reload of the application.
questions.explanation: Use flushSync sparingly when you need immediate DOM updates (e.g., for some third-party integrations).

questions.text: Which pattern is best for avoiding 'Prop Drilling' in a complex application?
options: State Lifting
Context API / State Management Libraries
Higher Order Components
Inline rendering
questions.explanation: Context allows data to be accessed at any level of the component tree without passing it through intermediate components.

questions.text: What is the 'Render-as-you-fetch' pattern enabled by Suspense?
options: Fetching data in useEffect after the component renders.
Starting a fetch at the same time you start rendering, without waiting for the response to begin the render phase.
Fetching data only when the user scrolls to a component.
Using a server-side cache for all API calls.
questions.explanation: This pattern reduces waterfalls by initiating requests as early as possible and letting React 'suspend' rendering while waiting.

questions.text: When implementing a 'Custom Hook', why is it important to ensure its internal hooks follow the 'Rules of Hooks'?
options: To make the code look cleaner.
Because custom hooks are just functions that inherit the rules of the React environment they are called in.
To allow the hook to be called inside loops.
Custom hooks don't actually have to follow the rules.
questions.explanation: Hooks must be called at the top level to maintain consistent state association across renders.

questions.text: What is the role of `useTransition` in React 18?
options: To animate route changes.
To mark certain state updates as non-urgent transitions, keeping the UI responsive.
To transition state between two different components.
To handle asynchronous CSS loading.
questions.explanation: Updates marked as transitions can be interrupted by more urgent work like user input.

questions.text: What is a 'Portal' and when would you use it?
options: A way to fetch data from another domain.
A method to render a component's children into a different DOM node (e.g., outside the parent's overflow:hidden).
A tool for creating multi-page applications.
A secure way to store passwords.
questions.explanation: Portals are commonly used for modals, tooltips, and floating menus.

questions.text: What occurs if a component 'Suspends' but there is no `<Suspense>` boundary above it?
options: The component renders as null.
React shows a console error and breaks the render tree.
React will look for the nearest boundary, and if none exists, it will crash the whole app/root.
React uses the default browser loading spinner.
questions.explanation: Suspense boundaries are required to catch suspended components; otherwise, the entire tree up to the root will fail to render.

questions.text: Which Profiler metric indicates how long a component took to render including its children?
options: Actual Duration
Base Duration
Commit Time
Start Time
questions.explanation: 'Actual Duration' measures the time spent rendering the subtree for the current update.

questions.text: What is the 'exhaustive-deps' lint rule intended to prevent?
options: Infinite loops caused by state updates inside effects.
Stale closures and bugs where effects don't sync with the latest props/state.
Large bundle sizes.
Duplicate code.
questions.explanation: Ensuring all used variables are in the dependency array keeps the effect synchronized with the component's render cycle.

questions.text: How do 'React Server Components' (RSC) differ from 'Client Components'?
options: RSCs run on the server and do not send JavaScript to the client.
RSCs are just class components.
Client components cannot use hooks.
RSCs can only be used with GraphQL.
questions.explanation: RSCs reduce bundle size by keeping logic and dependencies on the server, sending only the UI structure to the client.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `useId` hook?
options: To track user IDs.
To generate unique, stable IDs for accessibility attributes that match between server and client.
To set the 'key' attribute for list items.
To identify the current component in the DevTools.
questions.explanation: useId prevents hydration mismatches when generating IDs for form labels and aria-attributes.

questions.text: In a 'Compound Component' pattern, how is state typically shared between the parent and children?
options: Through props passed to every child.
Through an internal Context created by the parent component.
By using global state like Redux.
It isn't shared; children are independent.
questions.explanation: Context provides a clean way for sub-components (like Tabs.List and Tabs.Panel) to communicate without explicit prop-passing.

questions.text: What does the `useLayoutEffect` hook guarantee that `useEffect` does not?
options: It runs asynchronously.
It fires before the browser has a chance to paint the screen.
It is faster than useEffect.
It can be used in server-side rendering.
questions.explanation: useLayoutEffect is synchronous with DOM mutations, preventing visual flickers when reading/writing layout.

questions.text: What is 'Tree Shaking' in the context of a React build process?
options: Optimizing the DOM tree.
Removing unused code from the final JavaScript bundle.
Converting functional components to classes.
Deleting old files in the project.
questions.explanation: Tree shaking relies on ES6 module syntax to prune dead code, reducing the load time for the user.

questions.text: What is the primary trade-off of using `useMemo` extensively?
options: It makes the UI slower.
The overhead of memory usage and the cost of dependency comparison can sometimes outweigh the calculation savings.
It disables React's automatic batching.
It prevents hooks from working.
questions.explanation: Memoization isn't free; it should be used for expensive calculations or to maintain referential identity for optimization props.
